If anyone wishes to know a Gospel event, not only its main points and general outlines, but also its most minute and vivid details, it is to St. Mark that they should turn. One can easily imagine the freshness, the interest, the dramatic flair that such a work must possess. We must add that it also has an extraordinary rapidity; for St. Mark does not take much trouble to connect the events he recounts. He does not group them, like St. Matthew, according to a logical order: he simply links them together, most often in historical order, with the formulas καὶ, πάλιν, εύθέως. 2.1: Some time later, Jesus returned to Capernaum.
2.2: When word got around that he was in the house, so many people gathered there immediately that they could not find room even at the door, and he preached the word to them.
2.3: Then they brought him a paralytic carried by four men.
2.4: And, as they could not approach him because of the crowd, they uncovered the roof where he was and through the opening they lowered the stretcher on which the paralytic lay.
2.5: When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, «Son, your sins are forgiven.»
2.6: Now there were some scribes sitting there, thinking in their hearts:
2.7: «"How can this man speak like that? He is blaspheming. Who can forgive sins but God alone?"»
2.8: Jesus, immediately perceiving in his spirit that they were thinking this in their hearts, said to them, «Why do you think these things in your hearts?
2.9: Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, "Your sins are forgiven," or to say to him, "Get up, take your stretcher and walk"?
2.10: But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins,
2.11: »I command you,” he said to the paralytic, “get up, take your stretcher, and go home.”
2.12: And at that moment he got up, took his stretcher and went out in front of everyone, so that all the people were filled with wonder and gave glory to God, saying, "We have never seen anything like this."«
2.13: Jesus went out again along the sea and all the people came to him and he taught them.
2.14: As he passed by, he saw Levi, son of Alphaeus, sitting at the toll booth, and said to him, «Follow me.» Levi got up and followed him.
2.15: It happened that Jesus was having table in this man's house, and many tax collectors and sinners were eating with him and his disciples, for there were many who followed him.
2.16: The scribes and Pharisees, seeing him eat with sinners and tax collectors, said to his disciples, "Why does your teacher eat and drink with sinners and tax collectors?"«
2.17: Hearing this, Jesus said to them, «It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick; I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.»
2.18: Now John's disciples and the Pharisees were in the habit of fasting. They came to him and said, "Why is it that the disciples of John and those of the Pharisees fast, but your disciples do not fast?"«
2.19: Jesus answered them, «Can the guests of the bridegroom fast while the bridegroom is with them? As long as they have the bridegroom with them, they cannot fast.
2.20: But the days will come when the bridegroom will be taken away from them, and then they will fast in those days.
2.21: No one sews a new piece of cloth onto an old garment: otherwise the new piece takes a piece of the old one with it and the tear becomes worse.
2.22: And no one puts new wine into old wineskins; otherwise, the wine will burst the skins, the wine will spill out, and the skins will be ruined. But new wine must be put into new wineskins.»
2.23: It happened one Sabbath day that Jesus was passing through fields of wheat, and his disciples, as they went along, began to pick ears of grain.
2.24: The Pharisees said to him, «Now why are they doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath?»
2.25: He answered them, «Have you never read what David did when he and his companions were in need and hungry?
2.26: How did he enter the house of God in the days of Abiathar the high priest and eat the consecrated bread, which is only permitted to be eaten by the priests, and even give some to those who were with him?»
2.27: He also said to them, «The Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath,
2.28: That is why the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.»

3.1: Jesus entered the synagogue again, and there was a man there whose hand was withered.
3.2: And they watched him to see if he would heal him on the Sabbath day, so that they could accuse him.
3.3: Jesus said to the man with the withered hand, «Stand here in the middle,»,
3.4: Then he said to them, «Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to take it away?» And they remained silent.
3.5: Then, looking at them with anger and saddened by the blindness of their hearts, he said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." He stretched it out, and his hand was restored.
3.6: The Pharisees, having gone out, immediately conspired against him with the Herodians, to try to destroy him.
3.7: Jesus withdrew to the sea with his disciples, and a large crowd followed him from Galilee and Judea,
3.8: from Jerusalem, from Idumea and beyond the Jordan. Those from the vicinity of Tyre and Sidon, having heard of the things he was doing, also came to him in great crowds.
3.9: And he told his disciples to always have a boat at his disposal, so that he would not be pressed by the crowd.
3.10: Because he healed many people, all those who had any ailment rushed to touch him.
3.11: The unclean spirits, seeing him, bowed down before him and cried out, "You are the Son of God!",
3.12: but he forbade them, with great threats, to reveal his identity.
3.13: Having then gone up the mountain, he called those whom he himself wished, and they came to him.
3.14: He appointed twelve men to be with him and to be sent out to preach.,
3.15: with the power to cure diseases and drive out demons.
3.16: He gave Simon the nickname Peter.,
3.17: Then he chose James, son of Zebedee, and John, brother of James, to whom he gave the surname Boanerges, that is, sons of thunder,
3.18: Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddeus, Simon the Zealot,
3.19: and Judas Iscariot, who betrayed him.
3.20: They returned home and the crowd gathered there again, so that they could not even eat their meal.
3.21: When his parents learned of this, they came to seize him, for they said, "He has lost his mind."«
3.22: But the scribes who had come from Jerusalem said, "He is possessed by Beelzebul, and it is by the prince of demons that he casts out demons."«
3.23: Jesus called them together and told them in a parable: «How can Satan drive out Satan?
3.24: If a kingdom is divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand.,
3.25: And if a house is divided against itself, that house cannot stand.
3.26: If Satan rises up against himself, he is divided, he cannot subsist, and his power comes to an end.
3.27: No one may enter the fort's house and remove its furniture unless they first chain him up, and then they will plunder his house.
3.28: Truly I tell you, all sins will be forgiven the children of men, even the blasphemies they have uttered.
3.29: But whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven; he is guilty of an eternal sin.»
3.30: Jesus said this because they were saying, "He has an unclean spirit."«
3.31: When his mother and brothers arrived, they stood outside and sent word to him.
3.32: Now the people were sitting around him, and they told him, "Your mother and brothers are outside, looking for you."«
3.33: He replied, "Who is my mother and who are my brothers?"«
3.34: Then, looking around at those who were sitting around him, he said, «Here are my mother and my brothers.
3.35: For whoever does the will of God is my brother and sister and mother.»

4.1: Jesus began to teach again by the sea. Such a large crowd gathered around him that he got into the boat and sat in it on the sea, while all the crowd was on the shore.
4.2: And he taught them many things in parables, and in his teaching he said to them:
4.3: «Listen. The sower went out to sow.
4.4: And as he sowed, some seeds fell along the path, and the birds came and ate them.
4.5: Others fell on stony ground, where they did not have much soil, they stood up immediately, because the soil was shallow.
4.6: But when the sun rose, the plant, struck by its heat and having no roots, withered.
4.7: Other grains fell among the thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked them, and they did not produce fruit.
4.8: Others fell on good soil, growing up and increasing, they bore their fruit, some yielding thirtyfold, some sixtyfold, and some a hundredfold.»
4.9: And he added: «He who has ears to hear, let him hear.
4.10: When he was alone, those around him, with the Twelve, questioned him about the parables.
4.11: He said to them, «The knowledge of the mystery of the kingdom of God has been given to you, but to them who are outside, everything is spoken in parables,
4.12: "So that, seeing with their eyes, they may not see, nor hearing with their ears, they may not understand: lest they should turn and receive forgiveness of their sins."»
4.13: He added, "Do you not understand this parable? How then will you understand all parables?"
4.15: Those who are on the path are the men in whom the word is sown, and no sooner have they heard it than Satan comes and takes away the word sown in their hearts.
4.16: Likewise, those who receive the seed on rocky ground are those who, as soon as they hear the word, receive it with joy.,
4.17: But they have no root; they are fickle: when tribulation or persecution arises because of the word, they immediately fall away.
4.18: Those who receive the seed among the thorns are those who listen to the word.,
4.19: But the cares of the world, and the deceitfulness of riches, and other desires, entering into their hearts, choke the word, and it bears no fruit.
4.20: Finally, those whose seed fell on good soil are those who hear the word and accept it and produce a crop, thirty, sixty, or a hundredfold.»
4.21: He also said to them, «Is a lamp brought to be put under a bushel or under a bed? Is it not brought to be put on a lampstand?
4.22: For there is nothing hidden that will not be revealed, nothing done in secret that will not come to light.
4.23: If anyone has ears to hear, let them hear.»
4.24: And he added: «Be careful what you hear. According to the measure with which you have measured, it will be measured to you, and even more will be added to you.”.
4.25: For to everyone who has, more will be given, and from him who does not have, even what he has will be taken away.»
4.26: He also said: «The kingdom of God is like a man who scatters seed on the ground.
4.27: He sleeps and he gets up, night and day, and the seed germinates and grows, though he does not know how.
4.28: For the earth produces fruit of itself: first grass, then an ear of grain, and the ear is filled with wheat.
4.29: And when the fruit is ripe, the sickle is put in immediately, because it is harvest time.»
4.30: He also said, «To what shall we compare the kingdom of God? Or with what parable shall we describe it?”
4.31: It is like a mustard seed, which, when sown in the ground, is the smallest of all the seeds on earth.,
4.32: And when it is sown, it grows taller than all the other garden plants, and its branches spread so far that the birds of the air can find shelter in its shade.»
4.33: He taught them this way through various parables, as much as they were able to understand.
4.34: He did not speak to them without parables, but, in private, he explained everything to his disciples.
4.35: That day, towards evening, he said to them, "Let's cross to the other side."«
4.36: Having dismissed the crowd, they took Jesus with them, just as he was, in the boat, and other small boats accompanied him.
4.37: Then a violent whirlwind arose, pushing the waves against the boat, so that it was already filling with water.
4.38: He, however, was in the stern, asleep on the cushion; they woke him and said to him, "Master, do you not care that we are perishing?"«
4.39: Jesus, having awakened, rebuked the wind and said to the sea, "Be quiet, be calm." And the wind subsided and there was a great calm.
4.40: And he said to them, «Why are you afraid? Do you still have no faith?» And they were filled with great fear and said to one another, «Who then is this, that the wind and the sea obey him?»

5.1: Having crossed the sea, they arrived in the land of the Gerasenes.
5.2: And as Jesus was getting out of the boat, suddenly a man with an unclean spirit came to him from among the tombs.
5.3: He had his dwelling in the tombs and no one could keep him bound anymore, not even with a chain.
5.4: For he had often been bound with shackles and chains, and he had broken the chains and shattered his bonds, so that no one could control him.
5.5: Constantly, day and night, he wandered among the tombs and on the mountains, screaming and bruising himself with stones.
5.6: Having seen Jesus from afar, he ran up and knelt before him,
5.7: And, having cried out, he said in a loud voice, «What do you have to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I adjure you in the name of God, do not torment me.»
5.8: For Jesus had been saying to him, «Unclean spirit, come out of this man!»
5.9: And he asked him, "What is your name?" And he said to him, "My name is Legion, for we are many."«
5.10: And he earnestly begged him not to send them out of that country.
5.11: Now, there was a large herd of pigs grazing there along the mountain.
5.12: And the demons begged Jesus, saying, «Send us into those pigs, so that we may go in.»
5.13: He allowed them to do so at once, and the unclean spirits, coming out of the possessed man, entered the pigs, and the herd, which was about two thousand strong, rushed down the steep slopes into the sea and drowned.
5.14: Those guarding them fled and spread the news throughout the town and countryside. People went to see what had happened,
5.15: They came to Jesus and saw the demon-possessed man, the one who had had the legion, sitting there, clothed and sane, and they were terrified.
5.16: And those who had witnessed it told them what had happened to the possessed man and the pigs,
5.17: They began to pray to Jesus to leave their borders.
5.18: As Jesus got into the boat, the man who had been possessed asked permission to follow him.
5.19: Jesus did not allow him to do so, but said to him, «Go home to your family and tell them all that the Lord has done for you, and how he has had mercy on you.»
5.20: He went away and began to proclaim in the Decapolis all that Jesus had done for him: and all were amazed.
5.21: Jesus having crossed the sea again in the boat, as he was near the shore, a large crowd gathered around him.
5.22: Then came one of the synagogue leaders, named Jairus, who, upon seeing him, fell at his feet.,
5.23: and begged him earnestly, saying, «My daughter is dying; come, lay your hand on her, so that she may be healed and live.»
5.24: And he went with him, and a great multitude followed him and pressed around him.
5.25: Now there was a woman who had been suffering from a flow of blood for twelve years,
5.26: She had suffered greatly from several doctors and spent all her money, and far from experiencing any relief, she had seen her illness worsen.
5.27: Having heard about Jesus, she came into the crowd and touched the back of his cloak.
5.28: For she said, "If I only touch his clothes, I will be healed."«
5.29: The flow of blood stopped immediately and she felt in her body that she was cured of her infirmity.
5.30: At that moment, Jesus knew within himself that power had gone out from him, and turning around in the midst of the crowd, he said, "Who touched my clothes?"«
5.31: His disciples said to him, «You see the crowd pressing around you on all sides, and you ask, »Who touched me?’”
5.32: And he looked around to see who had touched him.
5.33: This woman, trembling with fear, knowing what had happened to her, came and threw herself at his feet and told him the whole truth.
5.34: Jesus said to her, «Daughter, your faith has saved you; go in peace and be healed of your infirmity.»
5.35: He was still speaking when someone came from the synagogue leader's house and told him, "Your daughter is dead; why burden the teacher any further?"«
5.36: But when Jesus heard this, he said to the synagogue leader, «Do not be afraid; just believe.»
5.37: And he did not allow anyone to accompany him except Peter, James, and John, the brother of James.
5.38: We arrive at the house of the synagogue leader and there he sees a confused group of people weeping and shouting loudly.
5.39: He went in and said to them, "Why all the noise and crying? The child is not dead, but asleep."«
5.40: And they mocked him. But he, having put them all out, took with him the child's father and mother and the disciples who were with him, and went into the place where the child was lying.
5.41: And taking her hand, he said to her, "Talitha qoumi," which means, "Young girl, get up, I tell you."«
5.42: Immediately the girl got up and began to walk, for she was twelve years old, and they were struck with amazement.
5.43: And Jesus strongly forbade them to tell anyone, then he told them to give the girl something to eat.

6.1: Having left there, Jesus came to his homeland and his disciples followed him.
6.2: When the Sabbath came, he began to teach in the synagogue, and many who heard him were amazed and said, «Where did this man get these things? What is this wisdom that has been given to him, and how are such miracles performed by his hands?”
6.3: Isn’t this the carpenter, the son of Mary, the brother of James, Joseph, Judah, and Simon? Aren’t his sisters here with us?» And he caused them to stumble.
6.4: Jesus said to them, «A prophet is not without honor except in his own town, in his own home, and among his own relatives.»
6.5: And he could not perform any miracles there, except that he healed some sick people by laying his hands on them.
6.6: And he was astonished at their unbelief. Then Jesus went around teaching from village to village.
6.7: Then he called the Twelve to him and began to send them out two by two, giving them authority over the unclean spirits.
6.8: He advised them to take nothing for the journey except a staff, no bag, no bread, and no money in their belts.,
6.9: but to wear sandals and not to wear two tunics.
6.10: And he said to them, «Wherever you enter a house, stay there until you leave that place.
6.11: And if somewhere they refuse to receive you and listen to you, leave and shake the dust from under your feet as a testimony to them.»
6.13: They drove out many demons, anointed many sick people with oil and healed them.
6.14: Now King Herod heard about Jesus, whose name had become famous, and he said, «John the Baptist has risen! That is why miraculous power is at work in him.»
6.15: But others said, "It is Elijah," and still others, "He is a prophet, like one of the prophets of old."«
6.16: When Herod heard this, he said, "John, whom I beheaded, has risen from the dead."«
6.17: For it was Herod who had sent and arrested John and put him in prison in chains because of Herodias, the wife of his brother Philip, whom he had married.,
6.18: For John had been saying to Herod, «It is not lawful for you to have your brother’s wife.»
6.19: Herodias was therefore hostile to him and wanted to kill him, but she could not.
6.20: For Herod, knowing that he was a just and holy man, revered him and watched over his life, did many things according to his advice and listened to him willingly.
6.21: Finally, a favorable opportunity presented itself. On the anniversary of his birth, Herod gave a feast to the nobles of his court, his officers, and the leading men of Galilee.
6.22: Herodias' daughter entered the hall, danced, and so pleased Herod and those at his table that the king said to the girl, "Ask me for whatever you want, and I will give it to you."«
6.23: And he added with an oath: "Whatever you ask of me, I will give it to you, up to half of my kingdom."«
6.24: She went out and said to her mother, "What shall I ask for?" Her mother replied, "The head of John the Baptist."«
6.25: Returning immediately and eagerly to the king, the young girl made this request: "I want you to give me, right now, on a platter, the head of John the Baptist."«
6.26: The king was saddened; nevertheless, because of his oath and his guests, he did not want to grieve him with a refusal.
6.27: He immediately sent one of his guards with orders to bring John's head on a platter.
6.28: The guard went and beheaded Jean in the prison and brought his head on a platter; he gave it to the girl, and the girl gave it to her mother.
6.29: When John's disciples heard about it, they came and took his body and laid it in a tomb.
6.30: Back with Jesus, the Apostles reported to him all that they had done and all that they had taught.
6.31: He said to them, «Come away by yourselves to a deserted place and get some rest.» For so many people were coming and going that the Apostles did not even have time to eat.
6.32: So they embarked and withdrew to a secluded place.
6.33: They were seen leaving and many people, having guessed where they were going, rushed by land from all the cities to that place and arrived before them.
6.34: When Jesus landed, he saw a great multitude and he had compassion on them, because they were like sheep without a shepherd, and he began to teach them many things.
6.35: As it was already late, his disciples came to him and said, «This is a deserted place, and it is already late,
6.36: Send them back so they can go to the surrounding farms and villages to buy themselves something to eat.»
6.37: He answered them, «You give them something to eat.» And they said to him, «Shall we then go and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread to give them to eat?»
6.38: He asked them, "How many loaves do you have? Go and see." When they found out, they told him, "Five loaves and two fish."«
6.39: Then he ordered them to have them all sit down, in groups, on the green grass.,
6.40: and they sat down in groups of a hundred and fifty.
6.41: Jesus took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, he gave thanks. Then he broke the loaves and gave them to his disciples to distribute to the people; he also divided the two fish among them all.
6.42: They all ate and were satisfied,
6.43: and they carried away twelve baskets full of pieces of bread and what was left of the fish.
6.44: Now those who had eaten numbered five thousand men.
6.45: Immediately afterwards, Jesus made his disciples get into the boat and go ahead of him to the other side of the lake, to Bethsaida, while he himself dismissed the people.
6.46: And after he had taken leave, he went up the mountain to pray.
6.47: When evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and Jesus was alone on land.
6.48: Seeing that they were having great difficulty rowing, because the wind was against them, around the fourth watch of the night he went towards them, walking on the sea and he wanted to pass them.
6.49: But when they saw him walking on the sea, they thought it was a ghost and cried out.
6.50: For they all saw him and were distraught. Immediately he spoke to them and said, «Take heart; it is I. Do not be afraid.»
6.51: He then got into the boat with them, and the wind ceased. Their astonishment was at its peak and they were beside themselves.,
6.52: because they did not understand the miracle of the loaves, because their hearts were blinded.
6.53: After crossing the lake, they came to the territory of Gennesaret and landed there.
6.54: When they got out of the boat, the local people immediately recognized Jesus,
6.55: They searched all the surrounding areas and began bringing the sick to him on stretchers, wherever they heard he was.
6.56: Wherever he arrived, in villages, in towns and in the countryside, the sick were put in public places and he was begged to let them just touch the fringe of his cloak, and all those who could touch it were healed.

7.1: The Pharisees and several scribes who had come from Jerusalem gathered around Jesus.
7.2: Having seen some of his disciples eating with unclean hands, that is, unwashed hands.
7.3: For the Pharisees and all the Jews do not eat unless they have carefully washed their hands, according to the tradition of the elders.
7.4: And when they return from the public square, they do not eat without having performed ablutions. They also observe many other traditional practices, such as the purification of cups, jugs, bronze vessels, and beds.
7.5: The Pharisees and the Scribes therefore asked him, «Why do your disciples not follow the tradition of the elders and eat with unclean hands?»
7.6: He answered them, «Isaiah was right when he prophesied about you hypocrites, as it is written: ‘These people honor me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me.’.
7.7: Their worship of me is in vain, teaching doctrines that are precepts of men.
7.8: You set aside the commandments of God and hold fast to human traditions, purifying vessels and cups and doing many other similar things.
7.9: You know very well, he added, how to thus nullify God's commandment, in order to observe your tradition.
7.10: For Moses said: Honor your father and your mother, and: Whoever curses his father and his mother, let him be put to death.
7.11: And you say: If a man says to his father or mother, "Whatever good I could have done for you is qorban," that is, a gift given to God,
7.12: You no longer allow him to do anything for his father or mother.,
7.13: "Thus nullifying the word of God by the tradition that you teach. And you do many other things like this."»
7.14: Having called the people together, Jesus said to them, «Listen to me, all of you, and understand.
7.15: Nothing that is outside a person can defile them by going into them, but what comes out of a person defiles them.
7.16: Let those who have ears hear well.»
7.17: When he had gone into a house, away from the crowd, his disciples asked him about this parable.
7.18: He said to them, «Are you so dull? Do you not understand that nothing that enters a person from the outside can defile them?”,
7.19: Because it does not enter his heart, but goes to his stomach and is expelled to the secret place, thus purifying all food?
7.20: But he added, what comes out of a man is what defiles him.
7.21: For it is from within, from the hearts of men, that evil thoughts, adultery, fornication, and murder come.,
7.22: theft, avarice, wickedness, fraud, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, madness.
7.23: All these evil things come from within and defile a person.»
7.24: He then left that place and went to the territories of Tyre and Sidon. And having entered a house, he wished that no one would know, but he could not remain hidden.
7.25: For no sooner had a woman, whose little girl was possessed by an unclean spirit, heard of him than she came and threw herself at his feet.
7.26: This woman was a pagan, of Syro-Phoenician nationality; she begged him to drive the demon out of her daughter.
7.27: He said to him, «Let the children be fed first, for it is not right to take the children’s bread and throw it to the dogs.»
7.28: »That’s true, Lord,” she replied, “but even little dogs eat children’s crumbs under the table.”
7.29: Then he said to her, "Because of this statement, go, the demon has left your daughter."«
7.30: Having returned home, she found her daughter lying on her bed; the demon had left her.
7.31: Leaving the country of Tyre, Jesus returned via Sidon to the Sea of Galilee, in the center of the Decapolis.
7.32: There, they brought a deaf-mute man to him and asked him to lay hands on him.
7.33: Jesus, pulling him aside from the crowd, put his fingers in his ears and spit on his tongue,
7.34: Then, raising his eyes to heaven, he sighed and said to her, "Ephphatha," meaning, open up.
7.35: And immediately the man's ears opened, his tongue loosened, and he spoke distinctly.
7.36: Jesus forbade them to tell anyone. But the more he forbade them, the more they proclaimed it.,
7.37: And filled with boundless admiration, they said, "Everything he has done is marvelous. He makes the deaf hear and the mute speak."«

8.1: In those days, when there was still a large crowd without food, Jesus called his disciples together and said to them:
8.2: «I have compassion for these people, for they have not left me for three days now and they have nothing to eat.
8.3: If I send them back to their homes without food, they will faint on the way, because many of them have come from far away.»
8.4: His disciples answered him, «How could we find enough bread here in the desert to feed them?»
8.5: And he asked them, "How many loaves do you have?" They said, "Seven."«
8.6: Then he made the crowd sit down on the ground, took the seven loaves and, after giving thanks, he broke them and gave them to his disciples to distribute, and they distributed them to the people.
8.7: They also had some small fish; after saying a blessing, Jesus had them distributed as well.
8.8: They ate and were satisfied, and seven baskets of the leftover pieces were taken away.
8.9: Those who ate were about four thousand. Then Jesus sent them away.
8.10: He immediately got into the boat with his disciples and came to the land of Dalmanutha.
8.11: Then the Pharisees arrived and began to argue with him, asking him for a sign from heaven to test him.
8.12: Jesus, having sighed deeply, said, «Why does this generation ask for a sign? Truly I tell you, no sign will be given to this generation.»
8.13: Leaving them, he got back into the boat and crossed to the other side.
8.14: But the disciples had forgotten to bring loaves of bread; they only had one with them in the boat.
8.15: Jesus gave them this warning: «Beware of the yeast of the Pharisees and of Herod.»
8.16: They discussed this among themselves, saying, "It's because we have no bread."«
8.17: Jesus, knowing their thoughts, said to them, «Why are you talking about having no bread? Do you still have neither sense nor understanding? Are your hearts still blinded?”
8.18: Do you have eyes but not to see, ears but not to hear? And do you have no memory?
8.19: "When I broke the five loaves among the five thousand men, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you take?" They told him, "Twelve."»
8.20: »And when I broke the seven loaves among the four thousand men, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you take up?« They told him, »Seven.”
8.21: He said to them, "How is it that you still don't understand?"«
8.22: They arrived at Bethsaida and a blind man was brought to him and asked him to touch him.
8.23: Taking the blind man by the hand, Jesus led him out of the village, put some of his saliva on his eyes and, having laid his hands on him, asked him if he saw anything.
8.24: The blind man looked up and said, "I see men walking around, like trees."«
8.25: Jesus again laid his hands on his eyes and made him look. Then he was so well healed that he could see everything clearly.
8.26: Then Jesus sent him home, saying, «Go home, but do not enter the village or tell anyone there about this.»
8.27: From there, Jesus and his disciples went to the villages around Caesarea Philippi, and on the way he asked them, «Who do people say I am?»
8.28: They answered him: "John the Baptist, others Elijah, others one of the prophets.
8.29: »But who do you say I am?« he asked them. »You are the Christ,” Peter answered.»
8.30: And he strictly forbade them to say that about him to anyone.
8.31: Then he began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders, the chief priests and the scribes, and that he must be killed and after three days rise again.
8.32: And he told them these things openly. Peter, taking him aside, began to rebuke him.
8.33: But Jesus turned and looked at his disciples and rebuked Peter, saying, «Get behind me, Satan! You are thinking not of God, but of men.»
8.34: Then, having called the people together with his disciples, he said to them, «If anyone wants to follow me, let him deny himself, take up his cross and follow me.
8.35: For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for me and for the gospel will save it.
8.36: What good will it be for a man to gain the whole world, yet forfeit his soul?
8.37: For what will a man give in exchange for his soul?
8.38: »Whoever is ashamed of me and my words in the midst of this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will also be ashamed of him when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels.”

9.1: He added, «Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God come with power.»
9.2: Six days later, Jesus took with him Peter, James and John and led them up a high mountain by themselves, and he was transfigured before them.
9.3: His clothes became dazzling, as white as snow, and such a whiteness that no launderer on earth could achieve.
9.4: Then Elijah and Moses appeared to them, talking with Jesus.
9.5: Peter, speaking up, said to Jesus, «Master, it is good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters, one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.»
9.6: He didn't know what he was saying, terror had seized them.
9.7: And a cloud covered them with its shadow, and from the cloud came a voice: «This is my beloved Son; listen to him.»
9.8: Immediately, looking all around, they saw no one except Jesus, alone with them.
9.9: As they were coming down the mountain, he ordered them not to tell anyone what they had seen until the Son of Man had risen from the dead.
9.10: And they kept it to themselves, wondering among themselves what the phrase "to be raised from the dead" meant.«
9.11: They questioned him and said, "Why then do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?"«
9.12: He answered them, «Elijah must come first and restore all things. And how is it written of the Son of Man that he must suffer many things and be despised?
9.13: But I tell you, Elijah has already come, and they did to him whatever they wished, just as it is written about him.»
9.14: Having returned to his disciples, he saw a large crowd around them and scribes who were discussing with them.
9.15: The whole crowd was surprised to see Jesus and immediately ran to greet him.
9.16: He asked them, "What are you discussing with them?"«
9.17: A man from the crowd answered him, "Master, I have brought you my son, who is possessed by a mute spirit.
9.18: Wherever the spirit seizes him, it throws him to the ground, and the child foams at the mouth and gnashes his teeth and withers away. I begged your disciples to drive it out, but they could not.
9.19: »O unbelieving generation,” Jesus said to them, “how long shall I be with you? How long shall I put up with you? Bring him to me.”
9.20: They brought him to her. At the sight of him, the spirit suddenly agitated the child violently; he fell to the ground and rolled around, foaming at the mouth.
9.21: Jesus asked the child's father, "How long has this been happening to him?" "Since he was a child," he replied.
9.22: Often the spirit has thrown him into the fire and into the water to make him perish; if you can do anything, have pity on us and help us.»
9.23: Jesus said to him, «If you can? All things are possible to him who believes.»
9.24: Immediately the child's father cried out, saying with tears, "I believe! Come to the aid of my unbelief!"«
9.25: When Jesus saw the crowds running together, he rebuked the unclean spirit, saying, «You deaf and mute spirit, I command you, come out of him and never enter him again.»
9.26: Then, giving a loud cry and shaking him violently, he came out, and the child became like a corpse, to the point that many said, "He is dead."«
9.27: But Jesus took him by the hand and helped him up, and he stood up.
9.28: When he had entered the house, his disciples asked him privately, "Why could we not drive out the spirit?"«
9.29: He told them, "This kind of demon can only be driven out by prayer and fasting."«
9.30: Having left there, they traveled through Galilee, and Jesus did not want anyone to know about it.,
9.31: For he was teaching his disciples, saying to them, «The Son of Man will be delivered into the hands of men, and they will kill him, and on the third day after his death he will rise again.»
9.32: But they did not understand this statement and they were afraid to question him.
9.33: They arrived at Capernaum. When he was in the house, Jesus asked them, «What were you talking about on the road?»
9.34: But they remained silent, for on the way they had discussed among themselves who was the greatest.
9.35: Then he sat down, called the Twelve together and said to them, «If anyone wants to be first, he must be the very last of all, and the servant of all.»
9.36: Then, taking a little child, he placed him in their midst and after kissing him, he said to them:
9.37: «Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.»
9.38: John, speaking up, said to him: «Master, we saw a man who does not go with us, driving out demons in your name, and we stopped him.
9.39: "Do not stop him," said Jesus, "for no one can perform a miracle in my name and then immediately speak ill of me.".
9.40: He who is not against us is for us.
9.41: For whoever gives you a cup of water in my name because you belong to Christ, I tell you truly, he will by no means lose his reward.
9.42: And whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to stumble, it would be better for him to have a millstone hung around his neck and to be thrown into the sea.
9.43: If your hand causes you to sin, cut it off: it is better for you to enter life maimed than to go into hell, into the unquenchable fire, with two hands.
9.44: where their worm does not die and where the fire does not go out.
9.45: And if your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off: it is better for you to enter life lame than with two feet to be thrown into hell of unquenchable fire.
9.46: where their worm does not die and where the fire does not go out.
9.47: And if your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out. It is better for you to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.,
9.48: where their worm does not die and where the fire does not go out.
9.49: For every man will be salted with fire, and every offering will be salted with salt.
9.50: Salt is good, but if it loses its saltiness, how can you make it salty again? Keep the salt within you, and be at peace with one another.»

10.1: Having left that place, Jesus came to the borders of Judea, beyond the Jordan, and the people gathered again around him, and, according to his custom, he began to teach them again.
10.2: The Pharisees approached him and asked if it was lawful for a husband to divorce his wife: this was to test him.
10.3: He answered them, "What did Moses command you?"«
10.4: They said, "Moses permitted the drawing up of a certificate of divorce and the sending away of a husband's wife."«
10.5: Jesus answered them, «It is because of the hardness of your heart that he gave you this law.
10.6: But at the beginning of creation, God made them male and female.
10.7: Because of this, a man will leave his father and mother and be devoted to his wife,
10.8: and the two will become one flesh.» So they are no longer two, but one flesh.
10.9: Therefore, what God has joined together, let no one separate.»
10.10: When they were in the house, his disciples asked him again about this.
10.11: And he said to them, «Whoever divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against the first wife.
10.12: And if a woman divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery.»
10.13: People brought little children to him so that he might touch them. But the disciples rebuked those who brought them.
10.14: When Jesus saw this, he was indignant and said to them, «Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these.
10.15: Truly I tell you, anyone who will not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it.»
10.16: Then he kissed them and blessed them, laying his hands on them.
10.17: As he was leaving to begin his journey, someone ran up and, throwing himself on his knees before him, asked him, "Good Master, what must I do to inherit eternal life?"«
10.18: Jesus said to him, «Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone.”.
10.19: You know the commandments: do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, abstain from all deceit, honor your father and mother.»
10.20: He replied, "Master, I have observed all these things since my youth."«
10.21: Jesus, having looked at him, loved him and said to him, «You lack one thing; go, sell all that you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me.»
10.22: But he, grieved by this saying, went away sorrowful, for he had great possessions.
10.23: And Jesus, looking around, said to his disciples, «How hard it is for those who have the goods of this world to enter the kingdom of God!»
10.24: As the disciples were astonished at his words, Jesus continued: «My little children, how hard it is for those who trust in riches to enter the kingdom of God.
10.25: It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.»
10.26: And they were even more astonished and said to one another, "Who then can be saved?"«
10.27: Jesus looked at them and said, «With man this is impossible, but not with God; for all things are possible with God.»
10.28: Then Peter spoke up: «Look,» he said, “we have left everything to follow you.”
10.29: Jesus answered, «Truly I tell you, no one will leave their house or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or fields for me and for the gospel,
10.30: that he may not now receive in this present time a hundred times as much: houses, brothers, sisters, mothers, children and fields, in the midst of persecutions and in the world to come, eternal life.
10.31: And many of the last will be first, and many of the first will be last.»
10.32: Now they were on their way up to Jerusalem, and Jesus was walking ahead of them. They were amazed and followed him with fear. Again Jesus took the Twelve aside and began to tell them what was going to happen to him:
10.33: «Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered over to the chief priests and the scribes, who will condemn him to death and hand him over to the Gentiles,
10.34: They will insult him, spit on him, flog him, and kill him, and three days later he will rise again.»
10.35: James and John, sons of Zebedee, approached him, saying, «Teacher, we want you to do for us whatever we ask of you.
10.36: »What do you want me to do for you?” he asked them.»
10.37: They said, "Grant us to sit, one at your right hand and the other at your left, in your glory."«
10.38: Jesus said to them, «You do not know what you are asking. Are you able to drink the cup I am going to drink, or to be baptized with the baptism I am going to be baptized with?»
10.39: They answered, «We can.» And Jesus said to them, «The cup that I am going to drink, you will indeed drink, and you will be baptized with the baptism with which I am baptized,
10.40: But to be seated at my right or my left, that is not for me to grant, except to those for whom it has been prepared.»
10.41: Having heard this, the other ten became indignant against James and John.
10.42: Jesus called them together and said to them, «You know that those who are recognized as rulers of the nations lord it over them, and that the great ones exercise authority over them.
10.43: It should not be so among you, but whoever wants to become great among you must become your servant.,
10.44: And whoever wants to be first among you must become a slave to all.
10.45: For the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.»
10.46: They arrived at Jericho. As Jesus was leaving that city with his disciples and a rather large crowd, the son of Timaeus, Bartimaeus the blind, was sitting by the roadside begging for alms.
10.47: Having heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to shout, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!"«
10.48: Many rebuked him to silence him, but he shouted all the more, «Son of David, have mercy on me!»
10.49: Then Jesus stopped and said, «Call him.» And they called him, saying, «Take heart; get up, he is calling you.»
10.50: Throwing off his cloak, he jumped up and came towards Jesus.
10.51: Jesus said to him, «What do you want me to do for you?» The blind man replied, “Rabbi, I want to see.”
10.52: Jesus said to him, «Go, your faith has saved you.» And immediately he saw him and followed him along the road.

11.1: As they approached Jerusalem, near Bethphage and Bethany, toward the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two of his disciples,
11.2: saying to them, "Go to the village ahead of you, and as soon as you enter it, you will find a donkey tied up, on which no man has ever sat: untie it and bring it to me.
11.3: And if someone asks you, »What are you doing?” answer, “The Lord needs him, and he will send him back here immediately.”
11.4: Having gone away, the disciples found a donkey tied to a gate outside, at the bend in the road, and they untied it.
11.5: Some of those who were there said to them, "What are you doing untying that donkey?"«
11.6: They answered as Jesus had commanded them, and they were allowed to do so.
11.7: And they brought the donkey to Jesus and put their cloaks on it, and Jesus sat on it.
11.8: Many spread their cloaks along the road, others, having cut branches from trees, strewn them across the path.
11.9: And those who walked in front and those who followed shouted: "Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!"
11.10: Blessed be the reign of David our father, which is about to begin! Hosanna in the highest!»
11.11: And he entered Jerusalem, into the temple, and having observed all things, as it was already late, he departed for Bethany with the Twelve.
11.12: The next day, after they had left Bethany, he was hungry.
11.13: Seeing a fig tree covered in leaves from afar, he approached to see if he could find any fruit there, and having approached it, he found only leaves, for it was not the season for figs.
11.14: Then he said to the fig tree, «May no one ever eat fruit from you again.» This is what his disciples heard.
11.15: They arrived in Jerusalem. Jesus entered the temple and began driving out those who were buying and selling there. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the seats of those selling doves.,
11.16: and he would not allow anyone to carry anything through the Temple.
11.17: And he taught, saying, «Is it not written: »My house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations’? But you have made it ‘a den of robbers.’”
11.18: Having heard this, the Princes of the priests and the Scribes sought ways to destroy him, for they feared him, because all the people admired his doctrine.
11.19: When evening came, Jesus left the city.
11.20: But when the disciples returned early in the morning, they saw the fig tree withered to the roots.
11.21: And Peter, remembering, said to Jesus, «Master, look, the fig tree that you cursed has withered.»
11.22: Jesus answered them, «Have faith in God.”.
11.23: I tell you truly, if anyone says to this mountain, “Go, throw yourself into the sea,” and does not doubt in their heart but believes that what they say will happen, it will be done for them.
11.24: Therefore, I tell you, whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you will receive it and you will see it come to pass.
11.25: When you stand praying, if you hold anything against anyone, forgive them, so that your Father in heaven may forgive you your sins.
11.26: If you do not forgive, your Father in heaven will not forgive your sins.»
11.27: They arrived back in Jerusalem. While Jesus was walking in the temple, the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders approached him.,
11.28: and said to him, «By what power are you doing these things? Who gave you authority to do them?»
11.29: Jesus said to them, «I too will ask you a question; answer me and I will tell you by what power I do these things.
11.30: Was John's baptism from heaven or from men? Answer me.»
11.31: But they were thinking to themselves, "If we answer, 'From heaven,' he will say, 'Why then did you not believe in him?'".
11.32: If we answer: men.» They feared the people, for they all considered John to be a true prophet.
11.33: So they answered Jesus, «We do not know. And I,» Jesus said, “will not tell you by what authority I do these things.”

12.1: So Jesus began to speak to them in parables. «A man planted a vineyard, put a hedge around it, dug a winepress in it and built a tower, then he rented it to some tenants and went on to another country.
12.2: When the time came, he sent a servant to the tenants to receive from them a share of the harvest.
12.3: But having seized him, they beat him and sent him away empty-handed.
12.4: He sent them yet another servant, and they wounded him in the head and heaped insults upon him.
12.5: He sent a third, whom they killed; many others were also killed, some beaten, others killed by them.
12.6: The master had one only son left, who was very dear to him; he sent him to them last, saying to himself: They will respect my son.
12.7: But these tenants said to one another, "This is the heir; come, let us kill him and the inheritance will be ours.".
12.8: And they seized him, killed him, and threw him out of the vineyard.
12.9: Now what will the owner of the vineyard do? He will come, he will destroy the tenants and give his vineyard to others.
12.10: Have you not read this passage of Scripture: “The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone”?
12.11: "The Lord did this, and it is marvelous in our eyes?"»
12.12: And they sought to seize him, knowing that he had them in mind in this parable, but they feared the people and left him, they went away.
12.13: So they sent some of the Pharisees and Herodians to catch him in what he said.
12.14: When they came to him, they said, «Teacher, we know that you are a man of truth and do not care about anyone, because you do not regard people’s appearances but teach the way of God in truth. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not? Should we pay or not?»
12.15: Knowing their treachery, he said to them, "Why are you tempting me? Bring me a denarius, so that I may see it."«
12.16: They brought it to him and he said to them, "Whose image and inscription are these?" "Caesar's," they said.
12.17: Then Jesus answered them, «Give back to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s.» And they were greatly astonished at him.
12.18: Then some Sadducees, who deny the resurrection, approached him and asked him this question:
12.19: «Master, Moses prescribed for us that if a brother dies, leaving a wife without children, his brother must take his wife and raise up children for his brother.
12.20: Now, there were seven brothers; the first took a wife and died without leaving any children.
12.21: The second man then took her and also died without leaving any children. The same thing happened to the third.,
12.22: And each of the seven took her and left no children. After all of them, the woman also died.
12.23: »Now then, in the resurrection, when they are resurrected, whose wife will she be? For all seven had her as their wife.”
12.24: Jesus answered them, «Are you not mistaken because you do not understand the Scriptures or the power of God?
12.25: For when men are raised from the dead, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven.
12.26: And concerning the resurrection of the dead, have you not read in the book of Moses, at the Passing Over the Burning Bush, what God said to him: I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob?
12.27: He is not the God of the dead, but of the living. You are therefore greatly mistaken.»
12.28: One of the scribes, who had overheard this discussion, seeing that Jesus had answered well, approached him and asked, "Which is the first of all the commandments?"«
12.29: Jesus answered him, «The first of all is this: Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.
12.30: You shall therefore love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your mind, and with all your strength. This is the first commandment.
12.31: The second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself. There is no other commandment greater than these.»
12.32: The Scribe said to him, «Well, Master, you have spoken the truth, that God is unique and that there is no other besides him,
12.33: And to love Him with all one's heart, with all one's mind, and with all one's strength, and to love one's neighbor as oneself, is more than all burnt offerings and sacrifices.»
12.34: When Jesus saw that he had answered wisely, he said to him, «You are not far from the kingdom of God.» And no one dared to ask him any more questions.
12.35: Jesus, continuing to teach in the temple, said, «How can the scribes say that the Christ is the son of David?
12.36: For David himself speaks thus by the Holy Spirit: The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.
12.37: David himself calls him Lord, how then can he be his son?» And the large crowd enjoyed hearing him.
12.38: He also told them in his teaching: «Beware of scribes who like to walk around in long robes, to receive greetings in public squares,
12.39: the best seats in the synagogues and the best places at feasts:
12.40: Those who devour widows' houses and ostentatiously offer long prayers will suffer a harsher condemnation.»
12.41: Having sat down opposite the trunk, Jesus observed how the people threw money into it; many rich people put in large sums.
12.42: A poor widow came and put in two small coins, together worth a quarter of an as.
12.43: Then Jesus called his disciples to him and said to them, «Truly I tell you, this poor widow has put more into the treasury than all the others.
12.44: For they all contributed from their surplus, but this woman gave from her poverty, everything she possessed, everything she had to live on.»

13.1: As Jesus was leaving the temple, one of his disciples said to him, "Master, look at these stones and buildings!"«
13.2: Jesus answered him, «Do you see these great buildings? Not one stone in them will be left on another; every one will be thrown down.»
13.3: When he sat down on the Mount of Olives opposite the temple, Peter, James, John, and Andrew questioned him privately:
13.4: «"Tell us when this will happen and what sign will it be that all these things are about to be fulfilled?"»
13.5: Jesus answered them and began this speech: «Take heed that no one deceives you.
13.6: For many will come in my name, saying, 'I am the Christ,' and they will deceive many.
13.7: When you hear of wars and rumors of wars, do not be alarmed, for these things must happen, but the end is not yet.
13.8: People will rise against people, kingdom against kingdom; there will be earthquakes in various places, and there will be famines. These will be the beginning of birth pains.
13.9: Be on your guard. You will be brought before the courts and the synagogues, and there you will be beaten. You will stand trial before governors and kings on account of me, to bear witness to me before them.
13.10: The Gospel must first be preached to all nations.
13.11: Therefore, when you are brought before them, do not think beforehand about what you are going to say, but say whatever is given you at that time, for it is not you who will be speaking, but the Holy Spirit.
13.12: Brother will betray brother to death, and father his son; children will rise up against their parents and put them to death.
13.13: And you will be hated by everyone because of my name. But the one who perseveres to the end will be saved.
13.14: When you see the abomination of desolation standing where it ought not to be, let the reader understand, then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains.
13.15: Let the one who is on the roof not go down into his house or enter to take anything out.
13.16: And let him who has gone into his field not return to get his cloak.
13.17: But woe to those who are pregnant or breastfeeding in those days.
13.18: Pray that these things do not happen in winter.
13.19: For in those days there will be tribulations such as have not been since the beginning of the world that God created until now, and never will be again.
13.20: And if the Lord had not shortened those days, no man would be saved, but he shortened them for the sake of the elect whom he chose.
13.21: If someone tells you then, "The Christ is here," or "The Christ is there," do not believe it.
13.22: For false Christs and false prophets will arise and perform signs and wonders, so as to deceive, if possible, even the elect.
13.23: For your sake, beware. See, I have told you everything in advance.
13.24: But in those days, after this tribulation, the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light,
13.25: The stars of heaven will fall, and the powers that are in heaven will be shaken.
13.26: Then we will see the Son of Man coming in the clouds with great power and great glory.
13.27: And then he will send his angels to gather his elect from the four winds, from the ends of the earth to the ends of heaven.
13.28: Listen to this comparison of the fig tree: As soon as its branches are tender and it puts out its leaves, you know that summer is near.
13.29: So when you see these things happening, you know that the Son of Man is near, he is at the door.
13.30: I tell you the truth, this generation will not pass away until all this happens.
13.31: Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away.
13.32: But about that day and hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.
13.33: Be careful, watch and pray, because you do not know when the time will come.
13.34: This is how a man, having left his house to go on a journey, after handing over authority to his servants and assigning each his task, commands the doorkeeper to keep watch.
13.35: Therefore, keep watch, because you do not know when the master of the house will come back—in the evening, or at midnight, or when the rooster crows, or in the morning.,
13.36: lest, coming suddenly, he might find you asleep.
13.37: What I say to you, I say to everyone: Watch.»

14.1: Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread were to take place two days later, and the chief priests and scribes were seeking ways to seize Jesus by trickery, in order to kill him.
14.2: «"But," they said, "it should not be during the festival, lest there be an uproar among the people."»
14.3: While Jesus was in Bethany, at the house of Simon the leper, a woman came in while he was reclining at the table. She had an alabaster jar full of very expensive perfume made of pure nard, and she broke the jar and poured the perfume on his head.
14.4: Several of those present expressed their displeasure: "Why waste this fragrance like this?"
14.5: "We could have sold it for more than three hundred denarii and given it to the poor." And they were angry with her.
14.6: But Jesus said, «Leave her alone. Why are you bothering her? She has done a good thing for me.”.
14.7: Because you always have the poor with you and whenever you want you can do good to them, but you don't always have me.
14.8: This woman did what she could; she embalmed my body in advance for burial.
14.9: »Truly I tell you, wherever this gospel is preached throughout the world, what she has done will also be told, in memory of her.”
14.10: Now Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, went to the chief priests to betray Jesus.
14.11: After hearing this, they rejoiced and promised to give him money. And Judas sought a favorable opportunity to betray him.
14.12: On the first day of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb was sacrificed, his disciples said to Jesus, «Where do you want us to go and prepare the Passover meal for you?»
14.13: And he sent two of his disciples and said to them, «Go into the city, and you will meet a man carrying a jar of water; follow him,
14.14: And where he enters, say to the owner of the house: The Teacher says to you: Where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?
14.15: And he will show you a large, furnished and ready-made meeting room: make the preparations for us there.»
14.16: His disciples left and went to the city and found things just as he had told them, and they prepared the Passover.
14.17: That evening, Jesus came with the Twelve.
14.18: While they were at the table eating, Jesus said, «Truly I tell you, one of you will betray me—the one who eats with me.»
14.19: And they began to grieve and to say to him, one after the other, "Is it I?"«
14.20: He answered them, "It is one of the Twelve, who puts his hand in the dish with me.
14.21: »As for the Son of Man, he will go, just as it is written about him. But woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed! It would have been better for that man if he had not been born.”
14.22: During the meal, Jesus took bread, and after saying a blessing, he broke it and gave it to them, saying, «Take it; this is my body.»
14.23: He then took the cup and, having given thanks, he gave it to them and they all drank from it.
14.24: And he said to them, «This is my blood, the blood of the new covenant, which is poured out for many.
14.25: Truly I tell you, I will not drink again of the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God.»
14.26: After the singing of the hymn, they went to the Mount of Olives.
14.27: Then Jesus said to them, «This very night I will cause you all to stumble, for it is written: ‘I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered.’.
14.28: But after I have risen, I will be your leader in Galilee.»
14.29: Peter said to him, "Even if you cause everyone to stumble, you never will cause me to stumble."«
14.30: Jesus said to him, «Truly I tell you, this very night, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times.»
14.31: But Peter insisted even more: "Even if I have to die with you, I will not deny you." And they all said the same.
14.32: They arrived at a place called Gethsemane, and he said to his disciples, "Sit here while I pray."«
14.33: And having taken with him Peter, James, and John, he began to feel fear and anguish.
14.34: And he said to them, «My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death; stay here and keep watch.»
14.35: Having moved forward a little, he threw himself to the ground and prayed that this hour, if it were possible, would pass from him.
14.36: And he said, «Abba, Father, all things are possible for you; yet take this cup from me; yet not my will, but yours be done.»
14.37: He then came and found his disciples sleeping and said to Peter, «Simon, are you sleeping? You could not have stayed awake for one hour.
14.38: Watch and pray so that you will not fall into temptation. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.»
14.39: And, going away again, he prayed, saying the same words.
14.40: Then, when he returned, he found them still asleep, for their eyes were heavy and they did not know what to answer him.
14.41: He returned a third time and said to them, «Sleep now and rest. It is enough. The hour has come; behold, the Son of Man is delivered into the hands of sinners.”.
14.42: "Get up, come on, the one who betrays me is near here."»
14.43: At that same time, while he was still speaking, Judas, one of the Twelve, arrived, and with him a large troop, armed with swords and clubs, sent by the Chief Priests, the Scribes, and the Elders.
14.44: The traitor had given them this sign: "The one I kiss is him; seize him and take him away under guard."«
14.45: As soon as he arrived, he approached Jesus, said, "Master," and kissed him.
14.46: The others threw their hands on him and arrested him.
14.47: One of those who were there drew his sword and struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his ear.
14.48: Jesus, speaking to them, said: «Have you come out as to a robber, with swords and clubs to take me away.
14.49: Every day I was among you, teaching in the temple, and you did not arrest me, but this was to fulfill the Scriptures.»
14.50: Then all his disciples abandoned him and fled.
14.51: A young man was following him, covered only by a sheet; they seized him.,
14.52: But he, letting go of the sheet, ran away completely naked.
14.53: They took Jesus to the high priest, where all the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders assembled.
14.54: Peter followed him at a distance, right into the courtyard of the high priest, and sitting down by the fire with the servants, he warmed himself.
14.55: However, the chief priests and the whole council were looking for evidence against Jesus to put him to death, but they could not find any.
14.56: For several people testified falsely against him, but the testimonies did not agree.
14.57: Finally, some stood up and gave this false testimony against him:
14.58: «We heard him say: »I will destroy this temple made with hands, and in three days I will build another, not made with hands.’”
14.59: But even on this point their testimonies did not agree.
14.60: Then the high priest stood up and came into the midst of them and questioned Jesus, saying, «Have you nothing to say to the charges these men are bringing against you?»
14.61: But Jesus remained silent and did not answer. The high priest questioned him again and said, «Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed One?»
14.62: Jesus said to him, «I am, and you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Almighty and coming with the clouds of heaven.»
14.63: Then the high priest tore his clothes and said, «What need have we of witnesses?
14.64: "You heard the blasphemy, what do you think of it?" They all declared that he deserved death.
14.65: And some of them began to spit on him and, covering his face, they struck him with their fists, saying to him, "Guess," and the guards beat him severely.
14.66: While Peter was downstairs in the courtyard, one of the high priest's servant girls came,
14.67: And seeing Peter warming himself, she looked at him and said, "You too were with Jesus of Nazareth."«
14.68: But he denied it, saying, "I neither know nor understand what you mean." Then he went away, reaching the vestibule, and the rooster crowed.
14.69: When the maid saw him again, she began to say to those present, "There's one of those people."«
14.70: And he denied it again. A little later, those who were there said to Peter, "You must be one of them, for you are a Galilean."«
14.71: Then he began to curse and swear an oath: "I do not know the man you are talking about."«
14.72: And immediately, for the second time, the rooster crowed. And Peter remembered the word that Jesus had spoken to him: "Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times," and he began to weep.

15.1: Early in the morning, without delay, the chief priests held a council with the elders and scribes and the whole Sanhedrin. And after binding Jesus, they led him away and handed him over to Pilate.
15.2: Pilate asked him, "Are you the king of the Jews?" Jesus answered him, "You say so."«
15.3: As the Princes of the Priests brought various accusations against him,
15.4: Pilate questioned him again, saying, "Have you no answer? See how many things they accuse you of."«
15.5: But Jesus made no further reply, so that Pilate was astonished.
15.6: However, at each Passover festival, he released a prisoner to them, the one they asked for.
15.7: Now, there was in the prison a man named Barabbas, with the seditious men who were his accomplices, for a murder they had committed in the sedition.
15.8: The crowd, having risen up, began to demand what he always granted them.
15.9: Pilate answered them, "Do you want me to release the King of the Jews to you?"«
15.10: because he knew that it was out of envy that the Princes of the priests had handed him over.
15.11: But the Pontiffs stirred up the people, in order to get them to release Barabbas instead.
15.12: Pilate, speaking again, said to them, "What then do you want me to do with the one you call the king of the Jews?"«
15.14: Pilate said to them, "But what wrong has he done?" And they shouted all the louder, "Crucify him!"«
15.15: Pilate, wanting to satisfy the people, released Barabbas to them and after having Jesus flogged, he handed him over to be crucified.
15.16: The soldiers led Jesus into the courtyard, that is, into the praetorium, and summoned the entire cohort.
15.17: And having clothed him in purple, they crowned his head with a crown of thorns which they had woven.
15.18: Then they began to greet him: "Hail, King of the Jews."«
15.19: And they struck him on the head with a reed and spat on him and, bending their knees, they paid him homage.
15.20: After mocking him in this way, they took off his purple robe, put his own clothes back on him, and led him away to be crucified.
15.21: A certain Simon of Cyrene, the father of Alexander and Rufus, passing by on his way back from the fields, was requisitioned by them to carry the cross of Jesus.,
15.22: that they lead to the place called Golgotha, which is translated as: place of the Skull.
15.23: And they offered him wine mixed with myrrh to drink, but he did not take it.
15.24: Having crucified him, they divided his clothes among themselves, casting lots to decide what each would take.
15.25: It was the third hour when they crucified him.
15.26: The inscription indicating the cause of his condemnation read: "The King of the Jews."«
15.27: They crucified two robbers with him, one on his right, the other on his left.
15.28: Thus was fulfilled the Scripture which says, «And he was numbered with the transgressors.»
15.29: Passersby insulted him, shaking their heads and saying, "Ah, you who destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days,
15.30: Save yourself and come down from the cross.»
15.31: The Princes of the priests, together with the Scribes, mocked him among themselves and said, "He has saved others, but he cannot save himself.".
15.32: "Now let Christ, the King of Israel, come down from the cross, so that we may see and believe." Even those crucified with him insulted him.
15.33: At the sixth hour, darkness came over all the land until the ninth hour.
15.34: And at the ninth hour, Jesus cried out with a loud voice, «Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani.» Which translates as, «My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?»
15.35: Some of those who were there, having heard it, said, "Look, he is calling for Elijah."«
15.36: And one of them ran and filled a sponge with vinegar, and having put it on the end of a reed, he gave him to drink, saying, "Leave it, let's see if Elijah will come and bring him down."«
15.37: But Jesus, having cried out with a loud voice, breathed his last.
15.38: And the veil of the sanctuary was torn in two, from top to bottom.
15.39: The centurion who stood opposite Jesus, seeing that he had died with such a cry, said, «Truly this man was the Son of God.»
15.40: There were also women watching from afar, including Mary Magdalene, Mary, mother of James the Less and of Joseph and Salome,
15.41: who had already followed him and served him when he was in Galilee, and several others who had gone up to Jerusalem with him.
15.42: Evening having come, as it was Preparation Day, that is to say, the eve of the Sabbath,
15.43: Then Joseph of Arimathea arrived: he was a highly respected member of the Sanhedrin, who was also waiting for the kingdom of God. He had gone courageously to Pilate to ask for the body of Jesus.
15.44: But Pilate, surprised that he had died so soon, summoned the centurion and asked him if Jesus had been dead for a long time.
15.45: Based on the centurion's report, he granted the body to Joseph.
15.46: Then Joseph, having bought a linen cloth, took Jesus down, wrapped him in the cloth and laid him in a tomb cut out of the rock, and rolled a stone against the entrance of the tomb.
15.47: Now Mary Magdalene and Mary, the mother of Joseph, were watching where he was laid.

16.1: When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices so that they might go and anoint Jesus.
16.2: And on the first day of the week, very early in the morning, they arrived at the tomb, the sun having already risen.
16.3: They said to one another, "Who will remove the stone from the entrance of the tomb?"«
16.4: And, raising their eyes, they saw that the stone had been rolled sideways; it was indeed very large.
16.5: Entering the tomb, they saw a young man sitting on the right, dressed in a white robe, and they were seized with fear.
16.6: But he said to them, «Do not be alarmed; you are looking for Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified: he has risen, he is not here, see the place where they laid him.
16.7: But go and tell his disciples and Peter that he is going ahead of you to Galilee; there you will see him, just as he told you.»
16.8: They immediately came out of the tomb and fled, for trembling and astonishment had seized them, and they said nothing to anyone because of their terror.
16.9: So Jesus, having risen on the morning of the first day of the week, appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons,
16.10: And she went and told those who had been with him, and they were grieving and weeping.
16.11: When they heard that he was alive and that she had seen him, they did not believe her.
16.12: Then Jesus appeared in another form to two of them as they were going into the country.
16.13: They returned to tell the others, who did not believe them either.
16.14: Later, he appeared to the Eleven themselves while they were at table and rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart, for not believing those who had seen him after he had risen.
16.15: Then he said to them, «Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
16.16: Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved; whoever does not believe will be condemned.
16.17: And these signs will accompany those who believe: In my name they will drive out demons, they will speak in new tongues,
16.18: They will pick up the snakes, and if they drink any deadly poison, it will not harm them; they will lay their hands on the sick, and the sick will get well.»
16.19: After speaking to them in this way, the Lord Jesus was taken up into heaven and sat at the right hand of God.
16.20: For them, having gone out, they preached everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming their word by the miracles that accompanied it.
